attributes.stem:
A 60-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-day history of subjective fever, malaise, dysuria and increased urinary frequency. She returned from an overseas holiday in Phuket, Thailand two weeks ago and was well at the time of her return. She did not have any pre-travel vaccinations but reports being very careful with food and drink during her trip. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is an active smoker with a 20 pack-year history. She denies alcohol or drug use. She has one regular male sexual partner. She reports no diarrhoea or abnormal vaginal discharge.

On examination, her pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 120/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 39.0°C. Her chest is clear on auscultation. Heart sounds are dual with no murmurs. Her abdomen is soft but she has left renal angle tenderness on palpation. There is no rash, jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Fever in the returned traveller requires careful assessment as it can represent both common conditions and rarer travel-associated diagnoses. A detailed history including travel details and activities, disease progression, examination findings and targeted investigations are often needed. In this case, the clinical presentation (fever, dysuria, increased urinary frequency, left renal angle tenderness) is most consistent with pyelonephritis, an ascending urinary tract infection that has reached the kidney.

Hepatitis A typically has an incubation period exceeding 21 days and presents with vomiting, jaundice and right upper quadrant discomfort. This patient's presentation is too early and lacks characteristic symptoms.

Malaria is uncommon in Phuket, does not cause urinary symptoms, and typically causes hepatosplenomegaly on examination. The timing and symptom profile do not fit.

Dengue fever has an incubation period of 3-10 days after a mosquito bite, so symptoms would have been expected earlier than two weeks post trip. It also doesn't typically cause urinary symptoms. While infections can be asymptomatic, symptomatic cases typically present with sudden onset high fever lasting 5-7 days, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgias and constitutional symptoms. Severe dengue, which is less common in Australia, typically develops around day 4-5 as fever settles, with warning signs including severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, lethargy, hepatomegaly, reduced oral intake, oliguria, postural hypotension and mucosal bleeding. Laboratory findings include increased haematocrit with concurrent rapid decrease in platelet count.

Influenza typically presents with prominent respiratory symptoms including cough, sore throat, and myalgia, which are absent here.

This case illustrates an important principle in assessing fever in returned travellers: common conditions remain common and may be unrelated to travel. While exotic causes must always be considered, the clinical presentation should guide the most likely diagnosis.
learningPoints: Common conditions remain the most likely diagnosis even in returned travelers with fever.
A systematic approach including travel history, disease progression, and examination findings helps differentiate between travel-related and common illnesses.
Fever with renal angle tenderness and urinary symptoms is highly suggestive of pyelonephritis requiring prompt antibiotic therapy.
references: Leggat PA. Assessment of febrile illness in the returned traveller. Australian family physician. 2007;36:328-32.

Liao W, Runnegar N. Rash in a returned traveller from Bali. Aust J Gen Pract. 2019 Jun;48(6):386-388. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-02-19-4861. PMID: 31220886. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/getattachment/7928d043-411d-4053-81a8-b3da664fccdc/Rash-in-a-returned-traveller.aspx
